title: LocoFormer: Generalist Locomotion via Long-context Adaptation
summary: LocoFormer is a generalist locomotion policy that controls diverse legged and wheeled robots zero-shot without precise kinematic knowledge. It is trained via massive-scale RL on procedurally generated robots with aggressive domain randomization and uses Transformer-XL to extend context length across episode boundaries. The policy adapts to unseen embodiments and dynamics at test time, showing emergent multi-episodic learning where early failures inform later control strategies.
key_contribution: A generalist locomotion framework that achieves cross-embodiment transfer through long-context adaptation spanning multiple episodes, enabled by large-scale RL on procedurally generated robots and Transformer-XL architecture with segment-level recurrence.
primary_method: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with Transformer-XL backbone for long-context in-context learning over procedurally generated robot morphologies
source_robot: procedurally generated legged and wheeled robots (bipeds, quadrupeds, wheeled variants)
source_task: locomotion with command tracking across varied morphologies and dynamics
target_robot: Unitree G1, Unitree H1, Unitree Go2, Unitree Go2-W (also tested in bipedal mode)
target_task: locomotion with adaptation to disturbances (knee locking, wheel locking, leg tying, added payloads)
weaknesses: Training is highly resource-intensive compared to specialist policies. The procedurally generated task space is hand-crafted, limiting scalability. The paper does not report quantitative real-world success metrics, relying primarily on qualitative case studies. The unified joint space approach may not scale to robots with significantly different kinematic structures beyond the tested morphologies.

title: Versatile Loco-Manipulation through Flexible Interlimb Coordination
summary: ReLIC (Reinforcement Learning for Interlimb Coordination) enables versatile loco-manipulation on legged robots by dynamically assigning limbs for either manipulation or locomotion based on task demands. The approach uses an adaptive controller with two modules: a model-based manipulation controller and an RL-trained locomotion policy that coordinates through shared state and limb assignment masks. Evaluated on 12 real-world tasks with a Spot quadruped equipped with an arm, ReLIC achieves 78.9% average success rate across diverse coordination patterns.
key_contribution: An adaptive controller architecture that seamlessly bridges manipulation and locomotion through flexible interlimb coordination, enabling dynamic limb role assignment without predetermined configurations or state machines.
primary_method: Hierarchical control with model-based manipulation module and RL-trained locomotion policy using contact-time gait regularization
source_robot: Boston Dynamics Spot with Spot Arm (simulation)
source_task: loco-manipulation with dynamic limb assignment (quadrupedal trotting, tripedal bouncing, coordinated arm-leg manipulation)
target_robot: Boston Dynamics Spot with Spot Arm and gripper
target_task: 12 loco-manipulation tasks (mobile interlimb coordination: Yoga Ball, Shipping Box; stationary interlimb coordination: Tire Pump, Trash Bin, Deck Box, Small Bin; foot-assisted manipulation: Tool Chest, Storage Bin, Chair, Basket, Drawer, Laundry Bag)
weaknesses: The high-level task interface based on contact points and language instructions operates in open-loop fashion, which can be brittle for tasks requiring real-time adaptation. The manipulation controller uses standard inverse kinematics, which may be insufficient for tasks requiring dynamic behaviors or collision avoidance. The approach is demonstrated only on arm-mounted quadrupeds; generalization to other morphologies (e.g., using arms for locomotion support) remains unexplored.

title: Real2Render2Real: Scaling Robot Data Without Dynamics Simulation or Robot Hardware
summary: Real2Render2Real (R2R2R) generates robot training data from smartphone-captured object scans and a single human demonstration video, without requiring teleoperation or physics simulation. The method uses 3D Gaussian Splatting to reconstruct objects, tracks 6-DoF motion from demonstrations, synthesizes diverse trajectories via interpolation, and renders thousands of robot demonstrations using IsaacLab. Policies trained on R2R2R data from one human demo match performance of models trained on 150 teleoperation demonstrations across five manipulation tasks on an ABB YuMi robot.
key_contribution: A scalable data generation pipeline that creates diverse robot training demonstrations from a single human video and object scan by combining 3D Gaussian Splatting reconstruction, trajectory tracking, kinematic trajectory synthesis via differential IK, and high-throughput mesh-based rendering, eliminating the need for teleoperation or dynamics simulation.
primary_method: 3D Gaussian Splatting for object reconstruction, 4D Differentiable Part Modeling for trajectory extraction, differential inverse kinematics for robot motion synthesis, and diffusion-based imitation learning (Diffusion Policy) or VLA finetuning (π0-FAST)
source_task: human manipulation demonstrations (pick up tiger, put mug on coffee maker, turn faucet off, open drawer, lift package with both hands)
target_task: autonomous execution of same manipulation tasks (pick up tiger, put mug on coffee maker, turn faucet off, open drawer, lift package with both hands)
weaknesses: The method relies on vision-based reconstruction that lacks watertight geometry and cannot model contact dynamics, limiting it to quasi-static prehensile manipulation. Trajectory synthesis uses geometric interpolation without collision avoidance, potentially generating infeasible paths in cluttered scenes. Grasp sampling is limited to antipodal grasps for parallel-jaw grippers. The system is vulnerable to tracking failures under fast motion, occlusion, or reflective surfaces. Policies still exhibit perception failures (missing handles, off-center grasps) that could benefit from wrist cameras.

title: ImMimic: Cross-Domain Imitation from Human Videos via Mapping and Interpolation
summary: ImMimic is an embodiment-agnostic co-training framework that learns robot manipulation policies from large-scale human videos and a small number of teleoperated robot demonstrations. The method uses Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) to map retargeted human hand poses to robot joints, then applies MixUp interpolation between paired human-robot trajectories to create intermediate domains for smooth domain adaptation. Evaluated on four real-world manipulation tasks across four robotic embodiments, ImMimic improves task success rates and execution smoothness compared to robot-only and vanilla co-training baselines.
key_contribution: A co-training framework that bridges the human-to-robot domain gap by using DTW-based mapping to pair human and robot demonstrations, followed by MixUp interpolation to create intermediate domains that enable smooth domain adaptation during joint training on both data sources.
primary_method: Diffusion-based imitation learning with DTW-guided MixUp interpolation for co-training on human videos and robot demonstrations
source_robot: human demonstrator (hand pose retargeting from videos)
source_task: manipulation tasks (Pick and Place, Push, Hammer, Flip) performed by humans
target_robot: Franka Emika Panda with four end-effectors: Robotiq 2F-85, Fin Ray, Allegro Hand, Ability Hand
target_task: autonomous execution of same manipulation tasks (Pick and Place, Push, Hammer, Flip)
weaknesses: The method shows inconsistent gains across embodiments, with some embodiment-task combinations achieving zero success rate despite using ImMimic (e.g., Hammer with Ability Hand, Flip with Allegro Hand). The paper attributes these failures to hardware design limitations but does not provide solutions. Performance is degraded under large domain gaps, and the method requires manual design of mapping strategies (action-based vs. visual-based). The paper lacks explicit training time and computational resource details, making reproducibility challenging.

title: Training Strategies for Efficient Embodied Reasoning
summary: This paper investigates why chain-of-thought reasoning improves vision-language-action (VLA) robot policies and proposes two lightweight training strategies (ECoT-Lite) that achieve similar performance gains without test-time reasoning overhead. The authors test three hypotheses—better representations, learning curriculum, and increased expressivity—finding that improved representations from learning to reason are the primary driver. The proposed methods achieve state-of-the-art results on LIBERO-90 with 3x faster inference than standard reasoning approaches.
key_contribution: Two practical training recipes (reasoning pre-training and reasoning dropout) that retain the performance benefits of robot chain-of-thought reasoning without requiring test-time reasoning generation, achieving 3x inference speedup while maintaining strong generalization.
transfer_mode: Unclear/NA - paper focuses on training strategies for VLA policies, not transfer learning; evaluates on same task distribution as training
primary_method: Vision-language-action models with chain-of-thought reasoning variants (pre-training, co-training, dropout, scaffolding, thinking tokens)
source_robot: demonstration data from LIBERO and BridgeData V2 datasets
source_task: diverse manipulation tasks with embodied reasoning annotations (plan, subtask, move rationales)
target_robot: Franka robot arm (implied from LIBERO and Bridge benchmarks)
target_task: same manipulation tasks with generalization tests (object position randomization, distractors, motion/spatial/semantic generalization)
weaknesses: The paper does not address the core limitation that robot reasoning still requires specialized reasoning data annotation, which can be expensive to obtain. The analysis of why certain training schemes work is largely speculative without fine-grained investigation of learning dynamics. The thinking tokens approach uses random token counts rather than optimized values. Limited to two environments (LIBERO and Bridge) with similar manipulation tasks.

title: Tactile Beyond Pixels: Multisensory Touch Representations for Robot Manipulation
summary: Sparsh-X is a self-supervised transformer-based backbone that fuses four tactile modalities (image, audio, accelerometer, pressure) from the Digit 360 sensor into unified touch representations. Trained on ~1M unlabeled contact interactions, it captures physical properties useful for manipulation. The paper demonstrates Sparsh-X's effectiveness in imitation learning for plug insertion, tactile adaptation of sim-trained policies for in-hand rotation, and benchmark tasks for inferring physical properties like object-action identification and force estimation.
key_contribution: The first multisensory touch representation backbone that fuses image, audio, motion, and pressure signals through self-supervised learning on ~1M contact interactions, enabling transferable touch perception for manipulation tasks.
primary_method: Self-supervised transformer with bottleneck fusion tokens for multisensory tactile representation learning, combined with attentive pooling for downstream task adaptation
source_robot: human demonstrator (kinesthetic teleoperation) for plug insertion; Hora policy trained in simulation for in-hand rotation
source_environment: simulation (for Hora base policy training); real-world lab environment (for demonstration collection and SSL pretraining data)
source_task: plug insertion demonstrations via kinesthetic teleoperation; proprioception-based in-hand rotation (Hora policy)
target_robot: Allegro hand with Digit 360 sensors (plug insertion); Allegro hand with Digit 360 sensors (in-hand rotation)
target_task: autonomous plug insertion; tactile-adapted in-hand rotation with improved stability
weaknesses: The tactile image modality shows lowest diversity in the pretraining dataset due to limited number of different sensor devices, potentially limiting generalization. Evaluation focuses on frozen representations without exploring fine-tuning benefits. Force estimation is limited to normal forces under controlled conditions and does not address shear forces or varying contact geometries. The paper does not explore how the representations scale with even larger and more diverse datasets.

title: Omni-Perception: Omnidirectional Collision Avoidance for Legged Locomotion in Dynamic Environments
summary: Omni-Perception is an end-to-end RL framework for legged robot locomotion that achieves omnidirectional collision avoidance by directly processing raw LiDAR point clouds. The system uses PD-RiskNet, a novel perception module that processes spatio-temporal LiDAR data through separate proximal and distal pathways, and includes a custom high-fidelity LiDAR simulation toolkit compatible with Isaac Gym, Genesis, and MuJoCo. The framework is validated on a Unitree Go2 robot in real-world experiments with static and dynamic obstacles.
key_contribution: An end-to-end locomotion policy that directly processes raw spatio-temporal LiDAR point clouds for omnidirectional collision avoidance, enabled by PD-RiskNet (a hierarchical perception module with proximal-distal risk assessment) and a high-fidelity LiDAR simulation toolkit with realistic noise modeling.
primary_method: Reinforcement learning with hierarchical point cloud processing (PD-RiskNet: proximal FPS+GRU with privileged height supervision, distal average downsampling+GRU) and risk-aware reward shaping
source_environment: simulation (Isaac Gym with custom LiDAR rendering)
source_task: velocity-tracking locomotion with omnidirectional collision avoidance in environments with static and dynamic obstacles
target_task: velocity-tracking locomotion with omnidirectional collision avoidance in environments with static and dynamic obstacles (rocky terrain, dense grass, aerial obstacles, moving humans)
weaknesses: Performance degrades in highly unstructured vegetation (dense grass) where LiDAR struggles to extract reliable geometric features. The sampling strategies used for sim-to-real transfer involve a trade-off that reduces fine-grained geometric detail, potentially limiting performance with very small or sparse obstacles. The paper lacks detailed ablation studies comparing different components of PD-RiskNet beyond the basic ablation in Table 3. Computational cost details (training time, GPU requirements) are not reported.

title: DemoSpeedup: Accelerating Visuomotor Policies via Entropy-Guided Demonstration Acceleration
summary: DemoSpeedup is a self-supervised method to accelerate visuomotor policy execution by speeding up the training demonstrations. It trains a proxy policy (ACT or Diffusion Policy) on normal-speed demonstrations to estimate per-frame action entropy, then segments trajectories based on entropy levels and down-samples high-entropy (casual) sections more aggressively than low-entropy (precision-demanding) sections. Policies trained on these accelerated demonstrations execute up to 3× faster while maintaining or improving task success rates.
key_contribution: A self-supervised entropy-guided demonstration acceleration method that identifies precision-demanding vs. casual segments in demonstrations and accelerates them at different rates, enabling faster policy execution without sacrificing performance.
transfer_mode: Unclear/NA - not a transfer learning paper; method accelerates demonstrations within the same domain
primary_method: Entropy-guided demonstration acceleration with nonparametric action entropy estimation via Gaussian kernel density estimation, followed by hierarchical density-based clustering (Hdbscan) for precision labeling and piecewise down-sampling
source_environment: real-world lab environment (Galaxea R1 bimanual humanoid platform)
source_task: bimanual manipulation tasks: Pen in Cup, Sort (yogurt bottles), Kitchenware (chopsticks/bowl/plate transfer), Bomb Deposal (wire detachment), Conveyer (scanner and bag manipulation)
target_task: same bimanual manipulation tasks as source, executed at accelerated speeds
weaknesses: The method occasionally causes minor performance drops due to dynamics mismatch between original and accelerated demonstrations. The desired acceleration rate must be manually determined per dataset since human operators collect data at varying speeds. The paper does not address DP inference delay which affects execution acceleration. Limited to tasks where precision requirements vary spatially within trajectories.

title: ReWiND: Language-Guided Rewards Teach Robot Policies without New Demonstrations
summary: ReWiND learns robot manipulation tasks from language instructions without per-task demonstrations. It pre-trains a language-conditioned reward function and policy from a small demonstration dataset, then fine-tunes the policy on unseen tasks using only language descriptions and the learned reward function. The reward model is trained with video rewinding augmentation and diverse Open-X data to generalize to new tasks and policy rollouts.
key_contribution: A framework that learns a generalizable language-conditioned reward function from limited demonstrations using video rewinding and diverse data augmentation, enabling sample-efficient policy adaptation to unseen language-specified tasks without new demonstrations.
primary_method: Language-conditioned reward learning with progress-based objectives, video rewinding augmentation, and offline RL (IQL) for policy pre-training
source_robot: Franka Panda (simulation) / human demonstrator (real-world data collection)
source_task: 15-20 demonstration tasks including door operations, object manipulation (Meta-World tasks); bimanual tasks like separating cups, folding towels (real-world)
target_robot: Franka Panda (simulation) / Koch v1.1 bimanual robot arms (real-world)
target_task: 8 unseen Meta-World tasks (e.g., door-close when trained on door-open); 5 real-world tasks including visual clutter (open red trash bin), spatial relationships (put orange cup on red plate), language variations (put fruit-colored object in box)
weaknesses: The method requires manual success signals during online RL, limiting full autonomy. The reward function architecture uses frozen encoders which may limit adaptation to very different visual domains. Real-world experiments use only 5 reward training demos but 10 policy demos, suggesting policy learning is a bottleneck. The paper does not deeply analyze failure modes of the reward function or provide theoretical guarantees on generalization.

title: Steering Your Diffusion Policy with Latent Space Reinforcement Learning
summary: DSRL proposes adapting pretrained diffusion policies by running reinforcement learning over their latent-noise space rather than modifying the policy weights. The method treats the diffusion policy as an action space transformation, enabling efficient online policy improvement without backpropagating through the multi-step denoising process. DSRL is evaluated on simulated benchmarks, real-world robotic manipulation tasks, and for adapting pretrained generalist policies like π₀.
key_contribution: A method for adapting diffusion policies by running RL over the latent-noise space, treating the diffusion policy as a black-box action space transformation, which avoids the computational challenges of finetuning diffusion policies directly.
primary_method: latent-noise space policy optimization with actor-critic methods (DSRL-NA variant uses noise aliasing for sample efficiency)
source_robot: Franka Panda (for multi-task BC policy experiments); π₀ generalist policy (for generalist policy experiments)
source_environment: simulation (for Robomimic, OpenAI Gym, Libero, AlohaTransferCube); Bridge V2 dataset (real-world demonstrations for multi-task policy)
source_task: behavioral cloning from demonstrations (Robomimic: Lift, Can, Square, Transport; multi-task: pick-and-place, drawer closing, block stacking; generalist: Libero pick-and-place, AlohaTransferCube)
target_robot: Franka Panda (for real-world multi-task experiments); WidowX 250 6-DoF (for Bridge V2 experiments)
target_task: online RL adaptation for pick-and-place, drawer closing, block stacking (real-world); toaster turning, spoon placement (π₀ real-world)
weaknesses: Exploration capabilities are inherently limited by the underlying diffusion policy's action distribution—highly concentrated policies may not provide enough diversity for effective steering. The method requires reward signals and online rollouts (for online RL variant), which introduces practical challenges. No theoretical characterization of which diffusion policies are steerable or how much improvement is possible. Real-world experiments still require resets.

title: ClutterDexGrasp: A Sim-to-Real System for General Dexterous Grasping in Cluttered Scenes
summary: ClutterDexGrasp presents a two-stage teacher-student framework for target-oriented dexterous grasping in cluttered scenes. A teacher policy is trained in simulation using clutter density curriculum learning and a novel safety curriculum, incorporating geometry- and spatially-embedded scene representations. The teacher's knowledge is distilled into a student 3D diffusion policy (DP3) that operates on partial point cloud observations, achieving zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for closed-loop grasping across diverse objects and layouts.
key_contribution: A two-stage teacher-student framework that enables zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for closed-loop target-oriented dexterous grasping in cluttered scenes through clutter density curriculum learning, comprehensive safety curriculum, and geometry-spatial scene representation.
primary_method: teacher-student framework with RL-trained teacher policy using clutter density and safety curriculum, distilled into 3D diffusion policy (DP3) for point cloud observations
source_task: target-oriented dexterous grasping in cluttered scenes with varying clutter densities (sparse: 4-8 objects, dense: 9-15 objects, ultra-dense: 16-25 objects)
target_robot: 7-DoF robotic arm with 12-DoF dexterous hand (real hardware)
target_task: target-oriented dexterous grasping in cluttered scenes (sparse: 9 scenes, dense: 5 scenes, ultra-dense: 3 scenes with 41 diverse objects)
weaknesses: The system struggles with tiny objects due to imprecise grasps from the sim-to-real gap and frequent occlusions. The paper lacks explicit training time and computational resource details. No comparison with other sim-to-real transfer methods or dexterous grasping baselines. The safety curriculum requires manual threshold tuning. Multi-view fusion or active vision strategies are not explored to improve perception in cluttered scenes.

title: Learning a Unified Policy for Position and Force Control in Legged Loco-Manipulation
summary: This paper proposes a unified reinforcement learning policy for legged robots that jointly models position and force control without requiring force sensors. The policy estimates external forces from historical robot states and compensates through position and velocity adjustments, enabling diverse manipulation behaviors including position tracking, force application, and compliant interactions. The learned force estimator is integrated into imitation learning pipelines, improving success rates by ~39.5% on contact-rich manipulation tasks.
key_contribution: The first unified policy for legged robots that jointly models force and position control learned without force sensors, enabling diverse control behaviors through a single policy and enhancing trajectory-based imitation learning with force-aware demonstrations.
primary_method: Reinforcement learning with impedance control formulation, combined with diffusion-based imitation learning for high-level policy that outputs force and position commands
source_robot: Unitree B2-Z1 quadrupedal manipulator and Unitree G1 humanoid robot
source_task: unified force-position control training with diverse combinations of position, velocity, and force commands under external disturbances
target_robot: Unitree B2-Z1 quadrupedal manipulator and Unitree G1 humanoid robot
target_task: force and position tracking, force-aware manipulation tasks (wipe-blackboard, open-cabinet, close-cabinet, open-drawer-occlusion), impedance control, force tracking
weaknesses: Force estimation accuracy degrades in high-frequency interactions and at workspace edges. Sim-to-real gap causes discrepancies in force accuracy along different coordinate axes. Current framework focuses on single-point force estimation; multi-point whole-body force coordination is left for future work. The paper does not explicitly model the reaction force F_react separately from external forces.

title: SAIL: Faster-than-Demonstration Execution of Imitation Learning Policies
summary: SAIL addresses the problem of executing imitation learning policies faster than the demonstration speed. The system integrates four components: consistency-preserving action inference using classifier-free guidance, high-fidelity tracking with a high-gain controller, adaptive speed modulation that slows down for critical actions, and action scheduling to handle system latencies. Experiments across 12 tasks in simulation and 7 real-world tasks on two robot platforms show up to 4× speedup in simulation and 3.2× in the real world.
key_contribution: A full-stack system enabling faster-than-demonstration execution of visuomotor policies through consistency-preserving action prediction, high-fidelity tracking of reached configurations, adaptive speed modulation, and latency-aware action scheduling.
primary_method: Diffusion Policy with classifier-free guidance for action consistency, combined with high-gain feedforward control and adaptive speed modulation
source_environment: simulation (RoboMimic benchmark, MimicGen) and real-world lab environment
source_task: manipulation tasks at demonstration speed (Lift, Can, Square, Stack, Mug Cleanup in sim; Stacking Cups, Wiping Board, Baking, Folding Cloth, Plate Fruits, Pack Chicken, Bimanual Serve in real)
target_environment: simulation (RoboMimic benchmark, MimicGen) and real-world lab environment
target_task: same manipulation tasks executed at higher speed (up to 4× in sim, 3.2× in real)
weaknesses: The system requires task-specific tuning of speed factors (c_slow, c_fast) and tracking error thresholds. The high-gain controller struggles with sustained contact dynamics (e.g., wiping task). The adaptive speed modulation relies on heuristics (motion complexity, gripper state) that may not generalize to all task types. The paper does not address transfer learning challenges—it focuses on execution speed within the same domain.

title: Data Retrieval with Importance Weights for Few-Shot Imitation Learning
summary: This paper introduces Importance Weighted Retrieval (IWR), a method for few-shot imitation learning that improves data selection from large prior datasets. IWR estimates importance weights using Gaussian kernel density estimates to compute the ratio between target and prior data distributions, addressing the high variance and bias issues of nearest-neighbor retrieval used in prior work. The method is evaluated in simulation and on the Bridge dataset, showing consistent improvements over existing retrieval-based approaches.
key_contribution: A retrieval method that uses Gaussian kernel density estimation to compute importance weights (the ratio between target and prior data distributions) for selecting relevant data from large prior datasets, improving upon nearest-neighbor retrieval by reducing variance and accounting for prior data distribution.
primary_method: Importance weighted retrieval using Gaussian kernel density estimation for data selection, combined with behavioral cloning
source_environment: real-world lab environment (Bridge dataset prior data)
source_task: diverse manipulation tasks from Bridge dataset (prior dataset with multiple tasks)
target_environment: real-world lab environment (Bridge dataset target tasks)
target_task: specific target manipulation tasks with limited demonstrations (few-shot regime)
weaknesses: The paper does not specify which robot platform was used in the Bridge dataset experiments, making it difficult to assess embodiment consistency. The method requires tuning bandwidth parameters for the KDE, which may require domain knowledge. The evaluation is limited to manipulation tasks and does not explore other domains like locomotion or navigation. The paper lacks ablation studies on the impact of different kernel choices beyond Gaussian.

title: AirExo
summary: AirExo-2 is a low-cost exoskeleton system for scalable in-the-wild demonstration collection, combined with visual and kinematic adaptors that transform collected data into pseudo-robot demonstrations. The paper also introduces RISE-2, a generalizable imitation learning policy that fuses 3D spatial and 2D semantic perception. Trained solely on adapted in-the-wild data from AirExo-2, RISE-2 achieves comparable performance to policies trained on teleoperated robot data.
key_contribution: A complete system combining a low-cost exoskeleton for in-the-wild demonstration collection with visual/kinematic adaptors that bridge the embodiment and visual gap, enabling policies to learn from pseudo-robot demonstrations without requiring physical robot data collection.
primary_method: diffusion-based imitation learning with dual encoders (sparse 3D geometric features and dense 2D semantic features) fused via spatial alignment
source_environment: in-the-wild real-world environments (diverse locations enabled by mobile base)
source_task: manipulation demonstrations (Collect Toys, Lift Plate, Open Lid, Close Lid, Serve Steak)
target_robot: dual-arm robot platform (specific model not named, but kinematically isomorphic to AirExo-2)
target_task: autonomous execution of same manipulation tasks
weaknesses: The visual adaptors require manual annotation for reference depth selection. The system currently only supports parallel grippers, limiting applicability to dexterous tasks. Calibrating in-hand cameras with the exoskeleton remains challenging. The depth adaptor relies on static reference depth which may not handle dynamic scene changes well. The paper does not explore integration with dexterous hands or additional sensor modalities (force/torque, tactile, sound).

title: π
summary: π0.5 is a vision-language-action model that enables mobile manipulators to perform long-horizon household tasks in entirely new homes. The model uses co-training on heterogeneous data sources including multi-robot action data, high-level subtask prediction, web data, and cross-embodiment demonstrations. It combines discrete token prediction during pre-training with flow matching for continuous action output during post-training, enabling hierarchical inference where high-level subtasks guide low-level action execution.
key_contribution: A co-training recipe that combines robot action data from multiple embodiments and environments, high-level semantic prediction, and web data to enable VLA models to generalize to entirely new real-world homes for complex mobile manipulation tasks.
primary_method: Vision-language-action transformer with hybrid discrete-continuous action representation, combining autoregressive next-token prediction with flow matching for action chunks
source_robot: Multiple robots: mobile manipulators (MM), non-mobile manipulators in diverse environments (ME), cross-embodiment lab robots (CE)
source_environment: Simulation and real-world: ~100 home environments for MM data, diverse home environments for ME, laboratory settings for CE, web data
source_task: Household manipulation tasks (cleaning, organizing, folding), bussing tables, folding shirts, plus high-level subtask prediction and web-based vision-language tasks
target_environment: Three entirely new real homes (kitchens and bedrooms) plus mock home environments not seen in training
target_task: Multi-stage household tasks: placing dishes in sink, putting items in drawer, putting laundry in basket, making bed (2-5 minute duration tasks)
weaknesses: The model still makes mistakes in new environments, particularly with unfamiliar object affordances (e.g., novel drawer handles), partial observability issues (arm occlusions), and high-level inference can get distracted (e.g., repeatedly opening/closing drawers). The paper does not report detailed failure analysis or provide quantitative metrics on what percentage of tasks succeed end-to-end. Prompt complexity is limited by training data. The computational cost of the full co-training recipe is substantial but not fully detailed.
